title: The Nicene Creed, Part 6
speaker: Mr. Steve Thomas
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 6 (Mr. Steve Thomas).txt
outline: Part 6 of the Nicene Creed series
Exposition of the relevant article(s) of the Nicene Creed
summary: Part 6 of the October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, presented by Mr. Steve Thomas. Continues the verse-by-verse exposition of the Creed in its historical and theological context.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Is the Church in the Creed?

Before we get to our topic, I want to try a quick exercise. Think to yourself about the five most important doctrines of the church — the five most important things. You don't need to say them out loud, just get them in your head.

Our topic for today is: *"And we believe in one holy Catholic and apostolic church."*

Before we get into that, let me do a quick review of where we have been. Last week, Mr. Alex Lyro spoke on the Holy Spirit proceeding from the Father and the Son, and there were real implications for how we live — things like living in unity with each other, husbands washing their wives in the word, and so on. If you don't remember those, go back and listen to that lesson again, because it is an important doctrine.

I also want to read the Nicene Creed in full before we begin, not just to take up time, but to set our topic in the context of the Creed at large. It is on page 846 in the small numbers of your hymnal:

> *"We believe in one God, the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth, and of all things visible and invisible. And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the only begotten Son of God, begotten of his Father before all worlds, God of God, light of light, very God of very God, begotten not made, being of one substance with the Father, by whom all things were made. Who for us and for our salvation came down from heaven and was incarnate by the Holy Spirit of the Virgin Mary and was made man. And was crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate; he suffered and was buried, and the third day he rose again according to the scriptures, and ascended into heaven, and is seated at the right hand of the Father, and he shall come again with glory to judge both the living and the dead, whose kingdom shall have no end. And we believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord and Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father and the Son, who with the Father and the Son is together worshiped and glorified, who spoke by the prophets. And we believe in one holy Catholic and apostolic church. We acknowledge one baptism for the remission of sins, and we look for the resurrection of the dead and the life of the world to come. Amen."*

Notice: the Creed has large sections at the front dealing with the Trinity — the Father, the Son, the Holy Spirit — and then a comparatively small section on the church, baptism, and a few other things. So we have to ask: why is this line in the Creed at all?

I asked you to think of your five most important doctrines. Did anyone include the doctrine of the church? My guess is most people did not — and I'm not saying that to call anyone out. It is probably fairly indicative of Western Christianity at large that the doctrine of the church doesn't make it into our top five or top ten. And yet it makes it into a formulation of the faith as basic as the Nicene Creed. Even things like personal piety, sanctification, the fellowship of the saints, and the Lord's Supper are not called out in the Creed the way the doctrine of the church is.

I lay out my conviction right here at the beginning: **the church is hugely important.** While passages like 1 Corinthians 15:3 make it clear that Christ died for us individually, passages like Ephesians 5:25 make it equally clear that Christ died for his church. Because Christ died for his church, the doctrine of the church needed to be included in the Creed.

## Two Preliminary Points

**First**, the doctrine of the church is closely linked with the doctrine of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit is our Helper who fills us, and as we think about the church, it is important that we think about it in its right relationship to the Holy Spirit — a community of believers who are called by the Spirit and sustained by the Spirit who fills them. We can see this linkage in the immediately preceding line of the Creed: "who spoke by the prophets" — that is the Holy Spirit. The preaching of the word is the normal means by which the Spirit calls believers, and the doctrine of the Holy Spirit and the doctrine of the church are closely linked.

**Second**, in the Creed we are saying that we *believe in* the church. What does that mean? We can all see the building is here. But I don't think that's what we mean.

I think we believe in the church in two primary ways. First, while the church may not live up to our expectations — or more importantly, may not live up to the expectations of Scripture — we are still called to believe in one holy Catholic and apostolic church. We believe in a communion of the saints over all times, a universal church, the body and bride of Christ, even when we cannot see that communion with our eyes. Second, we believe that when the church is truly one, holy, Catholic, and apostolic, we must give weight to its teaching. 1 Timothy 3:15 tells us that the church is "the pillar and ground of the truth." Paul writes: "If I am delayed, I write so that you may know how you ought to conduct yourself in the house of God, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and ground of the truth."

This does not mean we take church teaching at face value without examination. It does mean that we learn from the history of the church, and we give doctrinal formulations that have stood the test of time significant weight — then evaluate them through the lens of Scripture. I am not saying we take the historic teaching of the church as more important than Scripture. We are in the wrong branch of Christendom and the wrong month to be making that argument — we had the Reformation for a reason. But while we should examine everything diligently through the word, it should give us significant pause when we find ourselves differing doctrinally from historically orthodox positions, and we should look for clear biblical warrant when we do.

This is just Acts 17 — the Bereans searching the Scriptures. Pastor Tito Lyro referenced it on Wednesday; I was tempted to leave it out, but it is a great example of what we should be doing.

With all of that introduction, let us look at the adjectives before "church."

## One: Unity

By "one" we mean unity — two kinds of unity. First, internal spiritual unity. After we have spiritual unity, external visible unity becomes possible.

This unity can sometimes be hard to see. We are sitting in a Presbyterian church building, grateful for our doctrinal heritage and for like-minded believers. Thomas Witherow, an Irish Presbyterian, once said: "There is such a thing as being a Presbyterian without being a Christian, and it is possible to be a Christian without being a Presbyterian. Depend upon it, it's best to be both."

This quote cuts to the heart of what we're talking about. It is absolutely possible to be a Christian without being a Presbyterian, and absolutely possible to be a Presbyterian without being a Christian. What is important is that we are Christians — and yes, it is best to be both. We should strive for doctrinal purity, but that purity does not necessarily mean that people who don't believe the exact same things in the minutiae as we do are not Christians. We can cherish our understanding of the faith while still maintaining unity with all true believers.

We see this unity in our weekly prayers for our local sister churches — Slater-Kinney Baptist Church across the road, Emmanuel Bible Fellowship, Herbert Church, Reformation OPC, and many more. We have theological distinctives that differentiate us from each of those churches, but we recognize them as fellow believers in Christ, fellow saints, and fellow kingdom workers. As brothers and sisters in Christ, they — and all true believers across all times — are to be unified in their belief in one Lord, one faith, one baptism, and one God and Father of all. This is what we call the essential unity of the church.

Let us turn briefly to Ephesians 4:1–6:

> *"I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called, with all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called in one hope of your calling; one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all, who is above all, and through all, and in you all."* (Ephesians 4:1–6)

Paul is urging the believers in Ephesus to be unified. What is also important to note in context is that immediately preceding this passage, in Ephesians 3, Paul is explaining the great mystery of salvation — that the Gentiles are now fellow heirs of the same body and partakers of the same promise as the Jewish Christians, through the gospel. Despite the differences between the Jewish and Gentile communities — differences spanning centuries of different cultures, ethnicities, and a history of direct antagonism — both are saved through the same gospel. This gospel unity is far more important than anything that could divide them. The external unity demonstrated in Ephesians 4:1–6 is dependent on that prior doctrinal unity on the essentials of the faith: one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all.

When we do not share that core faith, we are not able to partake in this unity. For example: if an organization — and I am not calling it a church — were to teach that Christ was a created being who ascended into Godhood as we can, as Mormonism teaches; or if an organization denied the Trinity, as Unitarianism teaches; or if we were to deny the virgin birth and the literal, physical resurrection, as theological liberalism teaches — we would not expect external unity with those organizations, because we do not share central theological unity with them.

The Nicene and Apostles' Creeds are the ground floor of Christianity — we can believe more than these creeds and still be Christians, but we cannot believe less and still be Christians. None of the three organizations or movements I just mentioned would hold to these creeds in their literal meaning. Mormons and Unitarians would deny the historical creeds on doctrinal grounds. Theological liberals may hold to these creeds, but not in their literal sense — they will say they have allegorical or historical-contextual meanings. We are unified when we are holy, Catholic, and apostolic in our doctrine.

## Holy: Declared and Pursued

When we talk about the church's holiness, we mean two things: the church is *already* holy because Christ has made it holy, and Christ is *continuing* to make his church holy.

Even now, with its sins and hypocrisies, we are a holy people — set apart.

> *"But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, his own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light; who once were not a people but are now the people of God, who had not obtained mercy but now have obtained mercy."* (1 Peter 2:9–10)

These phrases — a royal priesthood, a holy nation, his own special people — are reminiscent of God's language for Israel in the Old Testament. They point out both the continuity between Israel and the New Testament church, and the holiness of God's people whom he has set apart and called holy.

Notwithstanding this declared holiness, we are to strive to be sanctified and to grow in holiness. A few verses later, in 1 Peter 2:11–12, Peter — having just called the church holy — continues to exhort the church to live holy lives: "abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul, having your conduct honorable among the Gentiles."

How is it that we are holy and yet still have to strive for holiness? That is how salvation works for us individually as well. God declares us holy; we are still responsible to wage war against the sin in our lives. The same applies to the church. We are holy; we cannot expect the church to be perfect; but we do have a responsibility to strive for holiness, both individually and corporately.

> *"Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for her, that he might sanctify and cleanse her with the washing of water by the word, that he might present her to himself a glorious church, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that she should be holy and without blemish."* (Ephesians 5:25–27)

Christ loved the church, gave himself for the church, so that he could sanctify her, cleanse her, and present her to himself without spot, blemish, or wrinkle. We are holy; we continue to strive to be holy through Christ's sanctification.

## Catholic: Four Aspects

At this point you might be thinking: we are Reformed — we had the Reformation. Let's just scrub this line and move on. That is not one of Martin Luther's 95 Theses, but let us talk about Catholicity.

The inclusion of the word "Catholic" does cause confusion if it is not adequately explained, and the concept of Catholicity is hugely important in a proper ecclesiology. First, the concept and even the usage of the word *Catholic* predates what we know as the Roman Catholic Church, and it does not mean the same thing Rome means when it speaks of Catholic. The word comes from the Greek *katholikos*, meaning general, universal, or pertaining to the whole of a thing.

It may seem troubling to include the word Catholic in this Creed because of its connotations — why not just replace it with *universal*? The word has been retained because it is a rich word with more to its meaning than simply "universal," and we should not let it be robbed of that richness just because it has been misused. As we look at the different ways the church is Catholic, hopefully it becomes clear why the word belongs.

The church's Catholicity has four important aspects:

**1. Geographical Catholicity.** Believers everywhere worship God in spirit and in truth. As Jesus taught the woman at the well, we no longer need to travel to Jerusalem to the temple. We worship him here, in spirit and in truth, and all other true believers worship in their places in spirit and in truth.

**2. Personal, Cultural, and Ethnic Catholicity.** Believers are united regardless of ethnic heritage, national origin, language, sex, or social standing. There are not multiple churches for Jews and Gentiles, for rich and poor, for black and white, for men and women, for Spanish-speaking and German-speaking. Practically we cannot worship with everyone across the face of the world all at once, but we do look forward to the time when we gather with all true believers from every tribe and tongue and people and nation to worship God, as Revelation tells us. The fact that we do not physically worship with those people right now does not undo that aspect of Catholicity — we are in the same universal church with people from every heritage.

**3. Temporal Catholicity.** Regardless of when true believers lived — whether in the New Testament age or even prior — we share the same faith, are saved through the same faith, and are united with them in Christ. This is why we can look into the Old Testament and find examples of faith, or turn to Hebrews 11 and the Hall of Faith, and draw great encouragement from those examples. Without the temporal Catholicity of the church, without the belief that all of us are members of the same church and saved in the same way, those examples of faith would have little meaning for us. But because we are members of the same church as they were, we can draw great encouragement from them.

**4. Orthodox Catholicity.** This phrase may not make intuitive sense given that the Orthodox Church and the Catholic Church are two large branches of Christendom that do not see eye to eye — you can ask Elder Nick Anderson about that. But if we understand these words in their generic meanings: we universally cling to orthodox doctrines. One of the earliest known usages of the word *Catholic* is from Ignatius of Smyrna, who wrote that those who denied that Christ really suffered — claiming he only *seemed* to suffer because he was purely a spiritual being, thus denying his humanity — were not part of the Catholic church. Likewise, one of the earliest definitions of Catholic is: *what has been believed everywhere, always, and by all*. In the earliest usages we have, the word actually refers to doctrine — to those original creeds and the apostolic faith that all of us must hold in order to be Christians. That is the last aspect in which the church is Catholic: we all hold those same foundational beliefs.

The word means more than just "universal" — or at least it means universal in a rich variety of aspects that the single word may not convey. If you have questions about the word's inclusion or are still confused by it, please come talk to me, or find one of the elders, or Pastor Tito Lyro when he returns.

A proper understanding of Catholicity helps us realize that we are pilgrims in a foreign land and that our citizenship is not in our time or place, but in the kingdom of heaven. As such, a proper understanding of the Catholicity of the church will necessarily help us avoid the pitfalls of, for example, racism — dividing along lines of ethnic heritage — or prioritizing national concerns over the concerns of the kingdom. As we view the church as universal and all-important, it necessarily causes us to be unified in the way Paul charges the church in Ephesus to be unified.

## Apostolic: Clinging to the Delivered Faith

The last adjective is *apostolic*, by which we mean that we continue in the faith delivered to us by the apostles.

As we study the book of Luke on Wednesday nights, we remember that both Luke and Acts are written to Theophilus. One of Luke's main goals in writing these books was to show the unity of Paul's message and Jesus' message — the continuity of the gospel — and to show that the messages Peter and Paul preached in the Jewish and Gentile worlds respectively are also unified. Thanks to the books of Luke and Acts, among others, we can be sure that the faith presented to us in the Gospels, the Epistles, and the rest of the New Testament is the same faith delivered by Christ to the apostles, and from the apostles to us.

Why does it matter that we cling to the same faith the apostles delivered? Because that is the faith they received from Christ — the true faith. Jesus tells us he is the way, the truth, and the life, and that no man comes to the Father except through him. If we believe a different faith from what Christ delivered to the apostles, we are not Christians. We do not have access to the Father, because no one comes to the Father except through Christ.

As we think about the apostolic faith, Paul's exhortation to the Galatians immediately comes to mind:

> *"But even if we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel to you than what we have preached to you, let him be accursed. As we have said before, so now I say again, if anyone preaches any other gospel to you than what you have received, let him be accursed."* (Galatians 1:8–9)

Paul cannot give stronger language than this to deal with those who preach a different gospel. Looking further through the New Testament, we see this theme throughout: the elder qualifications in Titus include the ability to refute bad doctrine and defend good doctrine; the Pastoral Epistles repeatedly tell Timothy and Titus to combat bad doctrine and cling to the sound doctrine they have received; Paul tells the churches again and again to cling to the faith delivered to them; and in John's Epistles, where Gnosticism is beginning to creep in, John says: no — cling to the faith we delivered to you.

The idea of the apostolic church simply means that we cling to the faith delivered to us from Christ through the apostles.

As we think about these four attributes — one, holy, Catholic, apostolic — it is easy to see how they are interlocking and overlapping. The church cannot be *one* if it is not both *holy* and *Catholic*. It cannot be *Catholic* without being unified and set apart in its beliefs. It cannot be *holy* as God is holy if it is not unified and Catholic. But it cannot be any of these things if we do not cling to the faith delivered to us by the apostles.

The apostolic faith is the lynchpin for these four adjectives. Without it, our holiness is a sham — we are set apart to nothing. We are unified in nothing. We are universal in nothing. Clinging to the true faith is of the utmost importance.

## The Westminster Confession on the Church

As we come to the end, I want to note that while the Nicene Creed gives us just five words of ecclesiology — "one holy Catholic apostolic church" — the Westminster Confession of Faith in chapter 25 gives us a more in-depth look at what we believe about the church. It is on page 863 in the small numbers of your hymnal, chapter XXV. I am not going to read it in full, but here are a few selected lines:

- "Under this Catholic visible church, Christ is given the ministry, oracles, and ordinances of God. Particular churches are more or less pure... there shall always be a church on earth to worship God."
- "This Catholic or universal church consists of the whole number of Christ's elect... under Christ the head thereof."
- "The visible church, which is also Catholic or universal under the gospel, consists of all those throughout the world who profess the true religion, and their children, and is the kingdom of the Lord Jesus Christ."
- "Out of which there is no ordinary possibility of salvation."

That last line is striking. The men who wrote our Confession were thoroughly convinced of the importance of the church and that it is the ordinary means of salvation. We must be part of a church.

## Conclusion and Application

Two final passages:

> *"Let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful. And let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is the manner of some, but exhorting one another, and so much the more as you see the Day approaching."* (Hebrews 10:23–25)

Holding fast to our confession and not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together are put right next to each other.

And from Ephesians 5:25 — just as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for her. While Christ died for us individually, he also clearly died for his church and is sanctifying his church. The writings of the New Testament could not be more clear on the importance of the church. Think about all the references to unity throughout the Epistles, all the references that deal with how the church is to conduct itself. The church was of primary importance to the apostles, and it should be of primary importance to us.

So that is the application for today: **be in church.** Be in church when you feel like it. Be in church when you don't feel like it. Especially when you don't feel like it — be in church.
